full_response:
We are given the coordinate transformation  

 t′ = x sinh(a t), x′ = x cosh(a t), y′ = y, z′ = z,  a = const. (1)

so (t′,x′,y′,z′) are the usual Cartesian (Minkowski) coordinates and (t,x,y,z) are the “Rindler”-like coordinates.

The curve Γ is defined in the un-primed system by keeping the spatial coordinates fixed,

 x = x0, y = y0, z = z0,  x0,y0,z0 = const.,  (2)

and letting the time coordinate vary.

Step 1:  Pick a parameter λ.  
Because the only coordinate that changes along Γ is t, the simplest and most natural choice is to let the parameter be that very coordinate:

 λ ≡ t. (3)

Step 2:  Write the parametric form of Γ in the un-primed system.  

 (t(λ), x(λ), y(λ), z(λ)) = (λ, x0, y0, z0). (4)

Step 3:  Express the same parametric curve in the primed (inertial) coordinates by inserting (4) into (1):

 t′(λ) = x0 sinh(a λ),  
 x′(λ) = x0 cosh(a λ),  
 y′(λ) = y0,  
 z′(λ) = z0. (5)

These equations describe the familiar hyperbola x′2 – t′2 = x0² in Minkowski space—the world-line of a uniformly accelerated observer with proper acceleration 1/x0.

(If one prefers to parametrize the curve by the proper time τ instead of t, note that along Γ one finds  
 ds² = –(ax0)² dt² ⇒ dτ = a x0 dt,  
so τ = a x0 t + const.  Either choice of parameter is acceptable; here we have chosen λ = t for simplicity.)

\boxed{\,\text{A convenient parametrization is } \lambda = t \;:\; (t,x,y,z)(\lambda) = (\lambda,\;x_{0},\;y_{0},\;z_{0})\;
\Longrightarrow\;
(t',x',y',z')(\lambda) = \bigl(x_{0}\sinh(a\lambda),\;x_{0}\cosh(a\lambda),\;y_{0},\;z_{0}\bigr).\,}

Confidence: 61%
